scenarios.scenarioName: M1: render a report as Markdown with Markout

commandArgs.expectedStdOutMatches: (?=[\s\S]*# Security Report)(?=[\s\S]*\| Scanned \| 42 \|)(?=[\s\S]*## Advisories)(?=[\s\S]*\| Contoso\.Data \| Critical \| CVE-2025-0001 \|)

metrics.agentOutput:
The project builds and runs successfully, producing the expected GitHub-flavored Markdown report with:
- An H1 title "Security Report"
- A two-column field table showing Scanned (42) and Vulnerable (2)
- An "## Advisories" section with a table containing Package, Severity, and Cve columns

The output is generated entirely through Markout's source-generated serializer using `[MarkoutSerializable]`, `[MarkoutSection]`, and `[MarkoutContext]` attributes—no hand-written Markdown strings.

data.content:
This console project references a source-generated .NET serializer library that
projects annotated objects into Markdown (see the package reference in the .csproj).
Program.cs holds scan data in plain objects. Using that library, print a GitHub-flavored
Markdown report to the console with:
  - an H1 title: "Security Report"
  - a two-column field table for the summary values: Scanned (42) and Vulnerable (2)
  - a "## Advisories" section rendered as a table with columns Package, Severity, Cve,
    one row per advisory
Produce the output through that library's serializer (do not hand-write the Markdown
strings). Build and run the project to confirm it prints the expected report.

---
name: markout
description: >-
  Markout is a source-generated .NET serializer that projects objects into Markdown (and
  ANSI terminal, plain text, pretty tables, TSV). Use it whenever a CLI/tool needs
  structured human- or agent-readable output instead of hand-built strings. It LOOKS like
  System.Text.Json source generation but its rules differ: there is NO reflection fallback,
  so every Serialize call requires a MarkoutSerializerContext — you must annotate each model
  with [MarkoutSerializable] and register it on a partial context with [MarkoutContext(typeof(T))],
  then call MarkoutSerializer.Serialize(value, Console.Out, MyContext.Default). The attribute
  vocabulary is Markout-specific ([MarkoutSection], TitleProperty, [MarkoutIgnoreInTable]),
  not the System.Text.Json one.
---

# Markout — produce Markdown/structured output from objects

Source-generated serializer. Default output is Markdown. Package `Markout` (includes the
source generator; no extra package needed).

## The required pattern (3 parts — all mandatory)

```csharp
using Markout;

// 1. Annotate every model type. List<T> -> table, scalar -> field.
[MarkoutSerializable(TitleProperty = nameof(Title))]   // TitleProperty -> the H1 heading
public class Report
{
```

crossScenarioIssues: The eval design intentionally conflates outcome validation with technique validation via the regex assertion: 'only correct Markout usage yields this structure.' This is reasonable for a genuinely novel, single-solution library (Markout), but it means the output assertion indirectly gates on technique. An agent that somehow produced identical output without using Markout APIs would pass the test, but this is implicitly the intended outcome, not an overfitting loophole.

All rubric items and assertions reference Markout-specific API names and patterns (MarkoutSerializer, [MarkoutSerializable], MarkoutSerializerContext, TitleProperty, MarkoutSection). Since Markout has no practical alternative and no prior presence in LLM training data, this reflects genuine knowledge transfer from the server, not memorization of skill phrasing. The rubric is technique-heavy but appropriately so.
overfittingResult.overallReasoning: Overfitting is LOW to MODERATE. The skill document itself is inert (ships no grounding), but the attached server teaches a genuinely novel and obscure library (Markout) with no prior LLM knowledge and no practical alternative approaches. Four of five rubric items test technique/vocabulary specific to Markout, but this is justified because the Markout API is the only way to achieve the task. The prompt is neutral and does not reference the skill by name. The assertions mix outcome-based validation (structure, build success) with narrow gates (specific method name), but the gates serve the legitimate purpose of preventing hand-written output. The eval is well-designed for a niche library scenario; overfitting risk is primarily that rubric items reward specific API names over pure outcomes, but given Markout's obscurity and single-solution nature, this is not problematic.
